text: The surgeon uses four robotic and two assistant trocars to perform the procedure. After retracting the liver, lymphadenectomy begins with segmented ATL lymph node dissection. The dissection continues along the left side of the hepatoduodenal ligament towards the TLA, followed by TLP dissection. The right gastric artery is clipped and divided, and lymphadenectomy proceeds along the right side of the hepatoduodenal ligament. The cholecystectomy is completed, aiding further lymph node dissection.

The surgeon divides the bile duct and sends the lower bile duct margin for frozen section evaluation. Further dissection reveals the tumor at the level of the hilum. The right hepatic artery is dissected, looped, and divided. Subsequently, the portal vein is looped to facilitate the dissection and isolation of the right branch of the portal vein.

The surgeon loops and ligates the right branch of the portal vein, applying a hemolog clip before dividing it. The right liver is then mobilized, and multiple short hepatic veins draining the caudate lobe are divided after being ligated with hemolyclips. Indocyanine green (ICG) is used to identify the demarcation line between the right and left lobes, which is marked with a cautery.

The surgeon performs liver transection using a combination of bipolar and monopolar devices. The Pringle maneuver is intermittently applied to minimize blood loss and aid in the dissection and identification of segmental hepatic veins draining into the middle hepatic vein. Dissection is carried out at the level of the hilum plate, and the bile duct is clamped and divided at the level of the left hepatic duct.

The surgeon mobilizes the left portion of the caudate lobe, controlling a large hepatic vein branch draining the superior aspect with bulldog clamps. This allows for complete separation of the caudate from the inferior vena cava (IVC) and the left lobe. The caudate is then mobilized to the right of the portal vein and IVC, facilitating an on-block caudate lobectomy.

The surgeon continues the liver transection cranially. The right hepatic vein is looped and divided using a vascular stapler, completing the right hepatectomy. A Roux limb of jejunum is transferred to the supracolic compartment, and a hepatico-jejunostomy is performed between the left hepatic duct and the jejunum using four polydioxanone sutures.
